items.title: The Java Module System with Sander Mak
items.summary: At J-Fall 2018 I talked to Sander Mak, modularity expert at Luminis, about the Java module system (J_MS), its adoption, how it compares to OSGi, and more.

items.title: Caliz III: Hashing scripts and background compilation
items.summary: Letting Caliz store native images and only create them for a given Java "script" (single source file) if needed

items.title: Jakarta EE, javax, And A Week Of Turmoil
items.summary: During a week of turmoil, many people have written about Jakarta EE and javax. This post summarizes the community's opinions and gives you plenty of links.

items.title: Caliz II: Wrapping Graal AOT
items.summary: Extending Caliz to create native images of Java "scripts" (single source files) with with Graal

items.title: Caliz I: Wrapping JVM 11 and learning about Graal AOT
items.summary: First steps toward an acceptable scripting experience with single-source-file execution and Graal native images

items.title: Definitive Guide To Java 12
items.summary: Detailed Java 12 guide: migration, versions; switch expressions, teeing collectors, indenting/transforming strings (and more); default CDS, Shenandoah, G1.

items.title: Teeing Collector in Java 12
items.summary: The teeing collector, available since Java 12 as Collectors::teeing, forwards its input to two other collectors before merging their results with a function.

items.title: Java 12 Experiments (Live Stream)
items.summary: In my first live stream ever (yay!), we explored Java 12's API improvements

items.title: Utilities, Singletons and Dependency Injection - Effective Java, Items 3-5
items.summary: Mildly surprising (to me), it makes sense to discuss these three patters in one video - so here it goes

items.title: Eleven Hidden Gems In Java 11
items.summary: Eleven small but shiny additions in Java 11 to classes like String , Path , Files , Collection , Optional , and others that make coding a little more elegant.

items.title: Fun with var
items.summary: A live-coding talk where I show off all you need to know about var in Java. And then some.

items.title: Scripting Java 11, Shebang And All
items.summary: On Java 11+, you can run a single source file without compiling it. Beyond experimentation, you can write scripts this way. Even shebang is supported!

items.title: Maven 3 / 4 / 5 with Robert Scholte
items.summary: Maven is one of the cornerstones of the Java ecosystem - here I talk with Robert Scholte, Chairman of the Apache Maven projects

items.title: Reactive HTTP/2 Requests And Responses In Java 11
items.summary: With Java 11's new reactive HTTP/2 API, request and response bodies can be handled with reactive streams: you can throttle, stream, and cancel early.

items.title: First Contact with Switch Expressions in Java 12
items.summary: With Java 12, switch is no longer just a statement, but becomes an expression. Let's take a look!

items.title: Java 11 HTTP/2 API Tutorial
items.summary: Tutorial for Java 11's new HTTP/2 API with HttpClient, HttpRequest, and HttpResponse at its center. Shows synchronous and asynchronous request handling.

items.title: Use Builders... Cautiously - Effective Java, Item 2
items.summary: Why and how to avoid the builder pattern and how to make best use of it if you can't

items.title: Java 11: A New Dawn - Releases, Oracle JDK vs OpenJDK, and LTS
items.summary: Oracle's announcements of the six-month release cadence and new licensing caused quite a ruckus - now that things calmed down, lets discuss where we're headed

items.title: All You Need To Know For Migrating To Java 11
items.summary: Migrating from Java 8 to Java 11? This has got you covered: licensing, long-term support, preparations, version requirements, migration challenges, and more.

items.title: Static Factory Methods - Effective Java, Item 1
items.summary: How to use static factory methods to overcome three shortcomings of constructors

items.title: Kicking off a series on Effective Java, Third Edition
items.summary: Kick-off to a YouTube series on Effective Java, Third Edition - let's find some angles Josh didn't cover

items.title: JUnit 5 Architecture or "What's Jupiter?"
items.summary: The JUnit 5 architecture promotes a better separation of concerns than JUnit 4 did. It also provides clear APIs for testers (Jupiter) and tools (Platform).

items.title: JUnit 5 Basics: @Test, Lifecycle, Assertions, Assumptions, And More
items.summary: The Basics of JUnit 5: How to use @Test , @BeforeAll , @BeforeEach , @AfterEach , @AfterAll , assertions, and assumptions. How to disable, name, and tag tests.

items.title: JUnit 5 Conditions: @Enabled, @Disabled, Customized
items.summary: A detailed look at JUnit 5's @Disabled , @DisabledOnOs , @DisabledOnJre , etc. and how to create custom conditions to flexibly disable test methods.

items.title: JUnit 5 - Dynamic Tests
items.summary: With JUnit 5's dynamic tests, we can create tests at run time, for example to parameterize tests, create hierarchical test plans, or define tests with lambdas.

items.title: JUnit 5 - Parameterized Tests
items.summary: Thorough introduction to parameterized tests in JUnit 5 with @ParameterizedTest, argument sources (eg @MethodSource, @CsvSource), and argument converters.

items.title: JUnit 5 Setup in IntelliJ, Eclipse, Maven, and Gradle
items.summary: How to set up JUnit 5 so tests run in IntelliJ, Eclipse, Maven, Gradle or, if all else fails, via JUnit 4 or on the command line.

items.title: JUnit 5 Extension Model: How To Create Your Own Extensions
items.summary: The JUnit 5 extension model enables detailed, flexible, and powerful additions to JUnit 5's core features. For that it provides specific extension points.

items.title: Unlocking Traits With var
items.summary: In Java 10, var makes it is possible to ad-hoc combine traits into an instance that matches your exact requirements. Alas, it has some downsides.

items.title: Tricks with var and anonymous classes (that you should never use at work)
items.summary: Local-variable type inference with var makes it easier to work with anonymous classes, e.g. for ad-hoc fields and methods. Don't do it, though!

items.title: Unlocking Intersection Types With var
items.summary: Java 10's var makes intersection types in Java more approachable. Generics tricks are still needed, but var makes it easy to declare such variables.

items.title: How To Use Multi-release JARs To Target Multiple Java Versions
items.summary: Multi-release JARs allow you to create a single JAR that contains bytecode for several Java versions with jar --version 9 (or 10, or...). Presented with a multi-release JAR, JVMs version 9 and later will load the code that was included for their version.

items.title: Code-First Java 9 Tutorial
items.summary: Showing code for the most important Java 9 features: private interface methods, stream and optional APIs, collection factories, reactive streams, stack walking, multi-release JARs, redirected platform logging, unified logging, the module system, and more. If you're new to Java 9, start here.

items.title: Goodbye 2017, Hello 2018
items.summary: 2017 draws to a close and 2018 is knocking. My annual review and preview went to my newsletter, so subscribe or head over to Medium to read them.

items.title: Maven on Java 9 and later - Six Things You Need To Know
items.summary: How to use the compiler executable, toolchains, and mavenrc to run Maven on Java 9 and how to use mvn/jvm.config and profiles to configure your build.

items.title: First Contact With 'var' In Java 10
items.summary: Java 10 introduces the var keyword, which lets the compiler infer local variable types. Here's how var works, why it exists, how it impacts readability.

items.title: First contact with var in Java 10
items.summary: How to use var , where it works and where it doesn't (and why), and how it might impact readability

items.title: Making JSR 305 Work On Java 9
items.summary: Using annotations from JSR-305 ( @Nonnull , @Nullable ) and javax.annotation ( @Generated , @PostConstruct ) on Java 9 causes a split package. Here's the fix.

items.title: Code-First Java Module System Tutorial
items.summary: Tutorial of Java 9 module system basics: declare modules with module-info.java, compile, package, launch them, learn of module path and readability graph.

items.title: Unified Logging Of JVM Messages With The -Xlog Option
items.summary: Java 9 introduces unified logging, a central mechanism configurable with -Xlog to observe class loading, threading, garbage collector, module system, etc.

items.title: Welcome, Java 9!
items.summary: Java 9 is out today and with other members of the community I'm throwing a welcome party. Get an all around view on the new Java release with various opinions, tips, and great sources!

items.title: Impressions of JavaZone 2017
items.summary: A few impressions of my stay at JavaZone 2017 to music from WillRock

items.title: Five Command Line Options To Hack The Java Module System
items.summary: Get your code running on the Java 9 Module System with the command line options --add-exports , --add-opens , --add-modules , --add-reads , and --patch-module .

items.title: Planning Your Java 9 Update
items.summary: A Java 9 update is not always trivial; quite the opposite, migrating to Java 9 can be challenging. Here's how to gather and categorize problems.

items.title: To JAR Hell And Back
items.summary: A live-coding talk where we take a typical Java 8 code base and update it to Java 9 and beyond, overcoming some common and some less common hurdles like dependencies on internal APIs and split packages

items.title: Java 9 Migration Guide: The Seven Most Common Challenges
items.summary: Solutions to the seven most common challenges for a Java 9 migration. Each of them explained with background, symptoms, and fixes.

items.title: A JDeps Tutorial - Analyze Your Project's Dependencies
items.summary: JDeps is a dependency analysis tool for Java bytecode (class files and JARs). Learn how to use filters, aggregate results, and create diagrams.

items.title: Java 9 Resources - Talks, Articles, Repos, Blogs, Books And Courses
items.summary: Java 9 draws and the number of posts and talks about it skyrocketed in the recent months. Here's a list of recommended talks and articles but also further resources where new, high-quality content will pop up.

items.title: Optional Dependencies with requires static
items.summary: The Java Platform Module System allows optional dependencies with requires static . They are accessible at compile but can be absent at run time.

items.title: Parameterized Tests in JUnit 5
items.summary: At JavaLand 2017, I spent 15 minutes exploring JUnit 5's (then) brand-new parameterized test feature in a NightHacking session

items.title: Repackaging Exceptions In Streams
items.summary: How to repackage checked exceptions that get thrown in a Java stream pipeline so that they can be thrown without the compiler complaining about it.

items.title: Expert Java 8
items.summary: With this talk, I help you get the most out of lambdas, Stream s, Optional s, and default methods, helping you master Java 8's core features

items.title: Why Elvis Should Not Visit Java
items.summary: The desire for the Elvis operator for easier null-handling echoes through the Java community. But due to Java's type system, it should never be introduced!

items.title: SPJCN IV: Quo Vadis Scala
items.summary: In the fourth issue of SitePoint’s Java Channel Newsletter (from October 21st 2016) I summarize the discussion of Scala's presumable demise.

items.title: Reflection vs Encapsulation
items.summary: Reflection wants to break into all code; encapsulation wants to give modules a safe space. How can this stand off be resolved?

items.title: Hello 2017!
items.summary: To finish all the projects I started in 2016, I will have to be disciplined and focused in 2017. Discipline and focus, what delightful words to start 2017.

items.title: Goodbye 2016, Sorry For Fucking Up
items.summary: Besides humanity as a whole fucking up 2016, it went well for me professionally. Maybe because I only cared about me? Possible...

items.title: SPJCN III: JavaOne 2016
items.summary: In the third issue of SitePoint’s Java Channel Newsletter (from October 7th 2016) I summarize JavaOne 2016 and recommend interesting talks to watch.

items.title: Publishing Snapshots With Gradle's maven-publish Plugin
items.summary: A step by step tutorial how to use Gradle and the maven-publish plugin to publish snapshots to Sonatype's Maven snapshot repo.

items.title: SPJCN II: What's Taking So Long?
items.summary: In the second issue of SitePoint’s Java Channel Newsletter (from September 23rd 2016) I wonder why Java 9 takes so long.
